SB1086.title: Zoning: allowed uses: microschools and micro-education entities.
SB1086.author: Megan Dahle
SB1086.summary: An act to add Section 65852.10 to the Government Code, relating to land use.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.
From printer. May be acted upon on or after March 16.

analysis.notes: SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10, which would require cities and counties to allow microschools and micro-education entities as permitted uses in zoning districts — overriding local land use decisions about where educational uses are appropriate. This constitutes a partial preemption of local zoning authority (Criterion A), likely mandates ministerial or by-right approval for qualifying micro-education uses (Criterion B/C), and shifts permitting and compliance administration costs to local governments without evident state funding (Criterion D).
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10 to require cities and counties to permit microschools and micro-education entities as allowed uses in local zoning, overriding local land use authority to determine where educational uses are appropriate.
• Preempts local zoning authority by mandating that microschools be treated as allowed uses regardless of a city's or county's general plan and zoning code designations.
• Likely eliminates or severely restricts local discretionary review — conditional use permits, design review, and neighborhood compatibility hearings — for qualifying micro-education facilities.
• Forces cities to approve a new category of institutional use that communities have not chosen to permit, effectively mandating development decisions beyond local preference.
• Shifts administrative and compliance costs onto local planning departments without identified state funding to offset the new permitting and enforcement obligations.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill explicitly overrides local zoning authority across all district types with no local opt-out or mitigation mechanism, and strips discretionary review for a new institutional use category cities have not chosen to permit.

analysis.comms_brief:
SB1003 adds state procedural requirements to development approvals, limiting conditions cities may apply and shortening the time available for local deliberation.
• Limits local conditions: restricts legitimate protective conditions cities attach to housing approvals, reducing mitigation authority for project impacts
• Mandatory timelines: short state deadlines for local response reduce capacity for thorough technical review, especially in complex or contested projects
• Compressed appeals: abbreviated local appeal processes limit community challenge opportunities for residents affected by nearby development decisions
Recommended: CSF should oppose; engage city engineers and attorneys to document workability concerns and impacts on local capacity.

AB1751.title: Missing Middle Townhome Ownership Act.
AB1751.author: Sharon Quirk-Silva
AB1751.summary: An act to add Section 65852.29 to, and to add Chapter 9 (commencing with Section 66499.45) to Division 2 of Title 7 of, the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 12.

analysis.notes: Creates a statewide ministerial pathway for townhomes on any residential parcel, directly preempting local zoning and eliminating all discretionary review; cities cannot require impact fees or infrastructure improvements for new townhome residents.
analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB1751 (Missing Middle Townhome Ownership Act) creates a statewide ministerial pathway for townhome development on any residential parcel, overriding local single-family zoning without community input.
• Cities lose all discretionary authority — projects meeting state objective standards must be approved regardless of neighborhood character, infrastructure capacity, or local planning
• Eliminates design review and public hearings for qualifying developments; no CEQA review applies
• No impact fee authority: cities cannot charge water, sewer, traffic, or school impact fees for new townhome residents — infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers
• Sets a precedent for further ministerial preemption of residential zoning across the state
Recommended: CSF should actively oppose; submit formal comment letters and coordinate with planning directors to document infrastructure and service capacity concerns.

SB1014.title: Development projects: onsite and offsite improvements.
SB1014.author: Tim Grayson
SB1014.summary: An act to add Sections 65913.3.2 and 65941.3 to the Government Code, relating to development projects.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.
From printer. May be acted upon on or after March 13.
Referred to Coms. on L. GOV. and HOUSING.

analysis.notes: The bill's title — "Development projects: onsite and offsite improvements" — and its addition of new Government Code sections (65913.3.2 and 65941.3) strongly suggest it limits the conditions cities may impose on developers regarding on- and off-site infrastructure improvements (e.g., road dedications, utility extensions, frontage work). This directly restricts local impact fee and improvement-condition authority, a hallmark local control risk. The pairing of the new sections with existing housing streamlining statutes (Ch. 65913 series) implies ministerial or by-right approval elements that reduce discretionary review and partially preempt local zoning standards for qualifying projects.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1014 adds two new Government Code sections that appear to restrict the onsite and offsite improvement conditions cities may impose on development projects, limiting local infrastructure requirements and reducing discretionary review authority for qualifying projects.
• Restricts cities' ability to require developer-funded offsite improvements (roads, utilities, frontage) as a condition of approval, shifting capacity costs to existing residents and local budgets.
• New sections in the 65913 streamlining series imply by-right or ministerial approval pathways that reduce or eliminate discretionary review, design review, and public hearing rights for covered projects.
• Partial preemption of local zoning and land use standards for qualifying developments, overriding community-determined conditions without a local opt-in.
• Infrastructure cost burden falls on existing taxpayers when cities cannot require developers to fund capacity improvements triggered by new projects.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill's placement in the housing streamlining code and its focus on limiting improvement conditions removes both local discretion and infrastructure cost recovery authority, with no apparent replacement mechanism for lost funding or review rights.

AB1997.title: Land use: housing development approvals: timelines and processes.
AB1997.author: Alex Lee
AB1997.summary: An act to amend Sections 65589.5, 65589.9, and 65950 of, and to add Section 65943.3 to, the Government Code, and to add Section 50411 to the Health and Safety Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 20.

analysis.notes: AB 1997 amends the Housing Accountability Act (65589.5), anti-NIMBY enforcement (65589.9), and permit streamlining timelines (65950), while adding new mandatory approval timeline provisions (65943.3) and a Health and Safety Code enforcement mechanism (50411). These sections collectively compress local review windows, restrict the conditions and findings cities may use to deny or modify housing applications, and impose state-level process mandates that override local deliberative authority — presenting meaningful local control risk across all four criteria even prior to committee amendments.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1997 amends the Housing Accountability Act and permit streamlining statutes to impose stricter mandatory timelines and approval processes on local housing decisions, narrowing the grounds and time available for cities to conduct meaningful review.
• Amendments to Government Code §65589.5 (HAA) further restrict the findings cities may use to deny, reduce, or condition housing projects, tightening state preemption of local zoning and design authority.
• New §65943.3 mandates specific approval timelines that compress or eliminate discretionary local review windows, limiting cities' ability to conduct thorough infrastructure, design, and community input processes.
• Amendments to §65950 (permit streamlining) and new Health & Safety Code §50411 layer additional procedural mandates and enforcement mechanisms, shifting compliance burden and litigation risk onto local governments without corresponding state funding.
• Collectively, these changes constrain impact fee conditioning, infrastructure review, and hearing rights for qualifying projects, potentially shifting capacity costs onto existing residents.
Recommended: Oppose — bill stacks multiple preemption mechanisms across HAA, permit streamlining, and new enforcement authority, compounding local control erosion with no offset for lost review authority or infrastructure conditioning rights.

AB2118.title: Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022: use by right: objective standards.
AB2118.author: Josh Hoover
AB2118.summary: An act to amend Sections 65912.101 and 65912.123 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 21.

analysis.notes: AB 2118 amends the Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022 (Gov. Code §§65912.101, 65912.123), which already establishes a use-by-right, ministerial approval pathway for qualifying affordable housing projects — explicitly overriding local zoning and eliminating discretionary review. The bill's amendment to "objective standards" language likely further constrains the conditions cities may apply to qualifying projects, tightening state preemption of local design, land use, and development review authority. Cities bear compliance and permitting costs with no identified state offset for ministerial processing burdens.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2118 amends the Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022 to modify the use-by-right, ministerial approval framework and the objective standards cities may apply to qualifying affordable housing projects, further narrowing local discretion.
• Use-by-right mandate strips cities of all discretionary approval authority — including design review, conditional use permits, and public hearings — for qualifying projects, regardless of local zoning or community plans.
• Amendments to "objective standards" language (§65912.123) risk further restricting the only remaining local tool for shaping project design and site compatibility, potentially invalidating locally adopted standards that exceed state floors.
• Ministerial approval pathway eliminates CEQA review for qualifying projects, removing cities' ability to analyze or condition projects based on local infrastructure, traffic, or environmental impacts.
• Cities bear unfunded administrative and permitting costs to implement mandatory ministerial processing with no state reimbursement mechanism identified.
Recommended: Oppose — bill tightens an existing preemption framework that already eliminates local discretionary authority, and further constrains the objective standards that are cities' last meaningful lever for project oversight.

AB2127.title: Accessory dwelling units.
AB2127.author: Natasha Johnson
AB2127.summary: An act relating to land use.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.

AB2288.title: Adaptive reuse: industrial zones.
AB2288.author: Sharon Quirk-Silva
AB2288.summary: An act to amend Sections 65658.1 and 65658.5 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 22.

analysis.notes: Forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial zones, preempting employment land protections; industrial zones lack residential utility infrastructure and cities cannot require developers to fund water, sewer, pedestrian, or parks upgrades needed for residential occupancy.
analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB2288 forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial-zoned areas, directly preempting employment land protections that communities have established through local planning.
• Preempts industrial land use designations: cities lose authority over employment zone preservation, undermining economic development strategies and tax base
• Threatens jobs and economic development: converting industrial to residential removes land cities rely on for tax-generating employment — a one-way transition with no reversal
• Infrastructure mismatch: industrial zones lack residential utilities, parks, and pedestrian infrastructure — cities cannot require developers to fund the needed upgrades for livable residential use
• No local environmental review: conversions proceed ministerially even on sites with contamination history or incompatible adjacent operations
Recommended: CSF should strongly oppose; partner with economic development organizations and labor groups on employment land preservation advocacy.

analysis.notes: AB 2295 adds Section 65584.2.3 to the Government Code, targeting the regional housing needs allocation (RHNA) process with a focus on affordable housing. Bills of this type typically adjust how RHNA allocations are calculated or distributed — potentially increasing the affordable unit quotas assigned to jurisdictions, which constitutes a moderate mandate on local planning beyond community preference and a partial limitation on local land use authority through state-controlled allocation formulas. Infrastructure burden is indirect, as higher affordable unit targets increase permitting and service-delivery pressure without explicit fee restrictions.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2295 amends the RHNA process by adding a new Government Code section focused on affordable housing allocations, likely increasing or restructuring the mandatory affordable unit quotas assigned to cities and counties through state regional planning bodies.
• RHNA quota increases directly override local planning choices by mandating cities accommodate more affordable units than they would otherwise zone for under local deliberation.
• State-controlled allocation formulas restrict local authority to determine appropriate density and affordability mix based on community needs and infrastructure capacity.
• Higher affordable housing targets impose indirect infrastructure and service-delivery pressure on cities without accompanying state funding or fee authority to offset costs.
Recommended: Monitor — bill is newly introduced with no committee analysis yet; risk level depends heavily on the specific allocation methodology added by the new code section, and full text review is warranted before escalating to Oppose.

AB2390.summary: An act to amend Section 65913.4 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.

analysis.notes: AB 2390 amends Government Code §65913.4 — the SB 35 streamlined ministerial approval statute — to extend its by-right approval process to projects on hazardous waste sites. This expands state preemption of local discretionary review to a site category that cities currently retain authority to condition or deny based on contamination concerns, public safety, and remediation adequacy. Cities could lose the ability to impose site-specific conditions tied to cleanup verification, and ministerial approval could force approvals before remediation is complete or certified adequate.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2390 expands SB 35's ministerial, by-right housing approval process to include hazardous waste sites, stripping cities of discretionary review authority over projects on contaminated land.
• Extends state preemption of local zoning and land use authority to a new, high-risk site category — hazardous waste locations — where cities have historically retained critical review rights.
• Eliminates or severely limits cities' ability to impose site-specific conditions tied to contamination remediation, cleanup certification, or phasing of development relative to cleanup progress.
• Removes discretionary review (design review, public hearings, conditional use permits) for qualifying projects on hazardous sites, reducing local tools for protecting public health and safety.
• Shifts potential infrastructure and mitigation costs to cities and existing residents if remediation standards or conditions cannot be imposed as part of the approval process.
Recommended: Oppose — ministerial approval on hazardous waste sites removes essential local public safety review with no replacement mechanism for cities to ensure adequate remediation before residents occupy new housing.

analysis.notes: AB 2433 amends Government Code Section 65915 — the core Density Bonus Law statute — and is titled an "affordable homes bonus," strongly suggesting it expands density bonus entitlements, streamlines approval, or broadens eligibility criteria. Amendments to §65915 typically preempt local zoning by requiring cities to grant additional density and reduced parking/development standards beyond what local plans allow, and often include ministerial or limited-discretion approval pathways for qualifying projects. analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2433 amends California's Density Bonus Law (Gov. Code §65915) to expand affordable homes bonuses, likely requiring cities to grant additional density entitlements and reduced development standards beyond what local zoning allows.
• Local Control Override: Expansions to §65915 are state preemptions — cities must grant bonuses regardless of local general plan or zoning designations, eroding community-driven land use decisions.
• Removes Discretionary Review: Density Bonus Law projects typically qualify for limited or ministerial review, restricting cities' ability to apply objective design standards or require full project-level deliberation.
• Mandates Development: Any broadening of eligibility or bonus tiers effectively forces cities to approve greater density than locally adopted plans would otherwise permit.
• Infrastructure Burden: Bonus density granted by state mandate may exceed infrastructure capacity that local impact fee conditions were calibrated to address, shifting costs to existing residents.
Recommended: Monitor pending full bill text — if bonus tiers or eligibility are substantially expanded, escalate to Oppose given cumulative preemption of local planning authority with no infrastructure relief mechanism.

analysis.notes: AB 2676 amends Section 66300 of the Government Code — the core anti-downzoning provision of the Housing Crisis Act of 2019 — which explicitly preempts cities and counties from reducing residential density, intensity of use, or the number of housing units permitted in any zone. analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2676 amends Government Code Section 66300 — the Housing Crisis Act of 2019's anti-downzoning core — which prohibits cities and counties from reducing residential zoning density or intensity, potentially expanding or entrenching state preemption of local land use authority.
• Amending the HCA's anti-downzoning provision risks broadening the existing prohibition on cities adjusting zoning standards, density limits, or permitted uses downward — removing a key tool for communities managing growth responsibly.
• HCA compliance already constrains the conditions and objective standards cities may apply to covered projects; any expansion of Section 66300's scope would further limit discretionary review authority.
• Increased anti-downzoning mandates add indirect pressure on local infrastructure capacity by preventing cities from calibrating density to existing water, sewer, and traffic systems.
Recommended: Oppose — the underlying statute already represents a significant local control override, and any amendment strengthening or expanding its reach warrants strong opposition until full bill text and intent are disclosed.

analysis.notes: SB 1353 amends Health & Safety Code §11834.23, which governs the residential use classification of alcohol/drug recovery and treatment facilities. Existing law typically requires cities to treat these facilities as residential uses in certain zones, limiting local zoning authority over their siting — an amendment in this space most likely adjusts or extends those state-imposed residential use mandates, constituting a moderate override of local land use classification authority (Criterion A) and a moderate mandate on what uses cities must permit in residential zones (Criterion C). The summary is thin and the bill is pre-referral; full text is needed to confirm the precise mechanism and direction of the amendment.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1353 amends the state law governing how cities must classify alcohol and drug recovery or treatment facilities as residential uses, potentially expanding or adjusting the state mandate that limits local zoning discretion over siting these facilities.
• Cities may be required to approve or permit these facilities in residential zones regardless of local zoning preferences or community input.
• State classification mandates remove local authority to apply land use conditions, spacing requirements, or density limits to these facilities.
• Expansion of residential use mandates could effectively override local general plan and zoning code designations without city or county consent.
Recommended: Monitor — bill text is too early-stage to confirm the direction of the amendment; if §11834.23 is broadened, opposition may be warranted on local control grounds.

analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1383 amends the postentitlement phase permit statute (Gov. Code §65913.3), further restricting city authority over building, grading, and encroachment permits issued after project entitlement.
• Tightened or expanded mandatory timelines limit cities' ability to use postentitlement permit processing as a backstop for unresolved land use, safety, or infrastructure concerns.
• Restrictions on conditions cities may impose at the postentitlement phase could prevent cities from requiring infrastructure improvements or corrections identified during permit review.
• Reduced local discretion at the postentitlement stage shifts compliance and capacity costs onto existing residents and city departments without guaranteed state funding.
Recommended: Monitor pending full text — if the bill eliminates or compresses current postentitlement review authority or restricts fee/condition imposition, escalate to Oppose.

analysis.notes: AB 748 requires cities and counties to establish a preapproved plan program for single-family and multifamily housing, compelling local governments to accept state-blessed prototype plans and process them ministerially — limiting local design review and objective standard application. This partially overrides local land use authority by mandating a new approval pathway, compresses discretionary review for qualifying projects, and imposes unfunded administrative burdens on local planning departments to develop, maintain, and administer the preapproval program.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 748 mandates that cities and counties establish and administer a preapproved housing plan program, creating a streamlined ministerial-style approval pathway that bypasses normal local discretionary review for qualifying single-family and multifamily projects.
• Partial preemption of local authority: cities must accept and process preapproved prototype plans, limiting their ability to apply locally-adopted design standards or require project-specific review.
• Removes discretionary review: qualifying projects submitted under preapproved plans are processed outside standard public hearing, design review, and conditional use permit processes.
• Mandates development approval: cities are effectively required to approve projects conforming to preapproved plans, reducing community choice over housing type and design in local neighborhoods.
• Unfunded compliance burden: local planning departments must build and maintain a new preapproval administrative infrastructure with no guaranteed state funding offset.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill imposes a mandatory preapproval program that erodes local design authority and generates unfunded administrative costs with no mechanism to recover expenses or preserve meaningful community input.

analysis.notes: SB 677 amends Government Code §65912.156, which sits within California's existing Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) streamlining framework. TOD statutes in this section typically mandate ministerial, by-right approval for qualifying housing projects near transit, overriding local zoning and eliminating discretionary review including CEQA. The amendment likely expands eligibility criteria, removes existing local conditions, or strengthens the ministerial approval mandate — all of which are strong local control overrides. Infrastructure burden is moderate because ministerial approvals under TOD law typically restrict the infrastructure conditions and objective standards cities may impose.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 677 amends California's transit-oriented development streamlining statute (Gov. Code §65912.156) to expand or strengthen mandatory ministerial approval of housing projects near transit, overriding local zoning, eliminating discretionary review, and limiting cities' ability to impose conditions.
• Explicit state preemption of local zoning: qualifying TOD projects must be approved ministerially regardless of what local plans or community standards allow
• Elimination of CEQA and discretionary review: cities lose the ability to conduct environmental review, hold public hearings, or apply design review to covered projects
• Mandatory development approval: cities are compelled to approve projects that meet state-defined criteria, removing local deliberation over density and housing type
• Infrastructure conditions constrained: ministerial approval pathways limit the infrastructure improvements and impact conditions cities may require, shifting capacity costs to existing residents
Recommended: Oppose — bill strips cities of zoning authority, eliminates public review, and mandates approvals without preserving local infrastructure protection tools; fetch full text recommended to confirm scope of §65912.156 amendment.

analysis.notes: AB 1710 amends Government Code §65589.5 — the Housing Accountability Act — and adds §65958, both of which are core state preemption mechanisms. Amendments to §65589.5 typically strengthen HAA's prohibition on local disapproval or conditioning of compliant housing projects, removing discretionary grounds cities may invoke. Adding §65958 likely layers additional mandates or streamlining requirements on local ordinances, policies, and standards, further limiting the conditions cities can impose on housing developments. Together these provisions represent explicit state preemption of local land use authority, elimination or severe restriction of discretionary review for qualifying projects, and effective mandates on cities to approve housing beyond what local deliberation would produce. Infrastructure review authority is also likely constrained to the extent cities cannot condition approvals on capacity or improvement requirements.
